description: Golden examples for answer-chain extraction quality. Numeric worked values may appear in model answers/checklists, but not reusable answerChain text.

cases.name: generic spring elastic energy chain is reusable

answerChain.title: Calculate elastic potential energy in a spring
answerChain.canonicalChainText: Convert the extension or compression into metres, use $E_e=\frac{1}{2}ke^2$, substitute the spring constant and extension or compression, calculate the energy, and give the unit.
answerChain.summary: Convert the extension or compression to metres, substitute into $E_e=\frac{1}{2}ke^2$, square the extension or compression, and give an energy unit.

steps.stepText: Convert the extension or compression into metres.
steps.explanation: The equation uses metres for extension or compression.
steps.commonOmission: Using the centimetre value directly as metres.

steps.stepText: Use $E_e=\frac{1}{2}ke^2$.
steps.explanation: This equation links elastic potential energy, spring constant and extension or compression.

steps.stepText: Substitute the spring constant and extension or compression into the equation.
steps.explanation: The extension or compression must be squared after substitution.
steps.commonOmission: Multiplying by the extension or compression instead of squaring it.

steps.stepText: Calculate the elastic potential energy.
steps.explanation: This gives the energy stored elastically in the spring.
steps.commonOmission: Rounding too early or omitting the factor of one half.

steps.stepText: Give the unit as J or joule.
steps.commonOmission: Giving N/m or N as the unit.

cases.name: worked spring solution is rejected as a chain

answerChain.title: Calculate elastic potential energy in a spring
answerChain.canonicalChainText: Convert the extension or compression into metres, use $E_e=\frac{1}{2}ke^2$, substitute the spring constant and extension/compression, calculate the energy, and give the unit joule.
answerChain.summary: Convert cm to m, substitute into $E_e=\frac{1}{2}ke^2$, square the compression, and give J.

steps.stepText: Convert $1.2\ \mathrm{cm}$ to $0.012\ \mathrm{m}$.
steps.explanation: The equation uses metres for extension or compression.

steps.stepText: Use $E_e=\frac{1}{2}ke^2$.
steps.explanation: This equation links elastic potential energy, spring constant and extension or compression.

steps.stepText: Substitute $k=8500$ and $e=0.012$ to get $E_e=0.5\times8500\times0.012^2$.
steps.explanation: The compression must be squared after substitution.

steps.stepText: Calculate $E_e=0.612$.
steps.explanation: This is the numerical elastic potential energy stored.
steps.commonOmission: Rounding too early or omitting the factor of one half.

cases.name: numeric model answer is allowed when chain is generic

answerChain.title: Calculate elastic potential energy in a spring
answerChain.canonicalChainText: Convert the extension or compression into metres, use $E_e=\frac{1}{2}ke^2$, substitute the spring constant and extension or compression, calculate the energy, and give the unit.
answerChain.summary: Use the elastic potential energy equation with consistent units.

steps.stepText: Convert the extension or compression into metres.
Substitute the known values into $E_e=\frac{1}{2}ke^2$.
Calculate the energy and give an energy unit.

cases.name: known numeric recall is review-only, not blocking

answerChain.title: Recall UK mains potential difference
answerChain.canonicalChainText: Recall that UK mains electricity has a potential difference of 230 V.
answerChain.summary: Use the standard UK mains potential difference.

steps.stepText: State that UK mains potential difference is 230 V.

cases.name: stable hash suffix in chain id is ignored

answerChain.title: Exchange surface adaptations increase diffusion efficiency
answerChain.canonicalChainText: Identify the adaptation, connect it to diffusion distance or surface area, and state how exchange becomes more efficient.
answerChain.summary: Use structure to explain faster exchange.

steps.stepText: Name the adaptation.
Link the adaptation to the diffusion pathway or surface area.

cases.name: algebraic formula constant is not a worked substitution

answerChain.title: Calculate final velocity using $v^2-u^2=2as$
answerChain.canonicalChainText: Convert units if needed, select $v^2-u^2=2as$, substitute the known quantities, then square-root to find the final velocity.
answerChain.summary: Use a constant-acceleration equation when distance and acceleration are known.

steps.stepText: Select $v^2-u^2=2as$.
Rearrange to make the unknown velocity the subject.

cases.name: generic-looking variable assignments are still worked substitutions

answerChain.title: Calculate force from mass and acceleration
answerChain.canonicalChainText: Select $F=ma$, substitute the mass and acceleration, and calculate the force.
answerChain.summary: Use Newton's second law with the known values.

steps.stepText: Substitute $m=2$ and $a=10$ into $F=ma$.

cases.name: alpha particle constants are review-only unless a daughter-specific substitution appears

answerChain.title: Balance an alpha decay nuclear equation
answerChain.canonicalChainText: In alpha decay, the emitted alpha particle has mass number 4 and atomic number 2. Balance the equation by conserving total mass number and total atomic number.
answerChain.summary: Use the alpha particle constants to balance the nuclear equation.

steps.stepText: Recall that an alpha particle is $^{4}_{2}\alpha$ or $^{4}_{2}\mathrm{He}$.
Use conservation of mass number and atomic number to find the missing nucleus.

cases.name: alpha decay daughter-specific arithmetic is rejected

answerChain.title: Balance an alpha decay nuclear equation
answerChain.canonicalChainText: In alpha decay, the emitted alpha particle has mass number 4 and atomic number 2. Balance the equation by conserving total mass number and total atomic number.
answerChain.summary: Use the alpha particle constants to balance the nuclear equation.

steps.stepText: Subtract 4 from 239 to get 235 for the daughter mass number.
